title: Recuperarea identității moldovene – cum tratează scriitorii contemporani din R. Moldova amintirile din comunism
summary: The article examines how contemporary Moldovan writers process Soviet-era trauma through literature, focusing on identity recovery and collective memory. It analyzes works by authors like Vladimir Beșleagă, Emilian Galaicu-Păun, and Dumitru Crudu, who explore linguistic, ethnic, and social identity struggles under the Soviet system. Their prose transforms personal and collective memories of totalitarianism into a literature of post-totalitarian recovery.
talking_points: Contemporary Moldovan novelists continue to thematize the struggle for linguistic, social, and ethnic identity within the Soviet system, forming a literature of post-totalitarian recovery

Soviet policies including deportations, famine, collectivization, Russification, and cultural isolation left deep scars on Moldovan collective memory that writers are still processing three decades later
The literary return to childhood is one of the most resonant and common creative strategies in contemporary Moldovan writing, serving as an act of identity reconstruction
Authors like Beșleagă pioneered resistance to socialist realism in the 1960s by transforming war experience into interior dramas of conscience and identity crisis

Writers explore how children growing up in Soviet families internalized contradictions between official ideology and authentic values, with small acts of rebellion representing fragile exercises in freedom
concepts: childhood as literary strategy for identity reconstruction
author: Oxana Gherman
